class Solution {
public:
    int nthUglyNumber(int n) {
        vector<long long> dp(n + 1);
        dp[0] = 1;
        int p2 = 0, p3 = 0, p5 = 0;

            long long next5 = dp[p5] * 5;

            if (nextUgly == next2) ++p2;
            if (nextUgly == next3) ++p3;
            if (nextUgly == next5) ++p5;
        }
        return (int)dp[n-1];
    }
};

class Solution {
public:
    int nthUglyNumber(int n) {
        vector<long long> dp(n + 1);
        dp[1] = 1;
        int p2 = 1, p3 = 1, p5 = 1;
        for (int i = 2; i <= n; ++i) {
            long long next2 = dp[p2] * 2;
            long long next3 = dp[p3] * 3;
            long long next5 = dp[p5] * 5;
            long long nextUgly = min(next2, min(next3, next5));
            dp[i] = nextUgly;
            if (nextUgly == next2) ++p2;
            if (nextUgly == next3) ++p3;
            if (nextUgly == next5) ++p5;
        }
        return static_cast<int>(dp[n]);
    }
};

But in C++, the vector<long long> dp(n+1) requires that n is at least 1. Since the constraints are 1<=n<=1690, that's okay.

But for n=1, the code returns dp[1], which is 1. Correct.

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int nthUglyNumber(int n) {
        vector<long long> dp(n + 1);
        dp[1] = 1;
        int p2 = 1, p3 = 1, p5 = 1;
        for (int i = 2; i <= n; ++i) {
            long long next2 = dp[p2] * 2;
            long long next3 = dp[p3] * 3;
            long long next5 = dp[p5] * 5;
            long long nextUgly = min(next2, min(next3, next5));
            dp[i] = nextUgly;
            if (nextUgly == next2) ++p2;
            if (nextUgly == next3) ++p3;
            if (nextUgly == next5) ++p5;
        }
        return static_cast<int>(dp[n]);
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: Uses dynamic programming with three pointers to generate each subsequent ugly number by multiplying previous values with 2, 3, and 5. Selects the minimum of these candidates to ensure ordered generation. Efficiently avoids redundant calculations and maintains linear time complexity. The main weakness is potential integer overflow for very large n, though the problem constraints ensure values fit within 64-bit integers.
